curriculum.subject: Physics
curriculum.overallDescription: 19 units, 28 chapters, and 14 topics extracted through raw extraction, document-structure hierarchy building, node enrichment, normalized teaching blocks, validation, and statistics generation.
coreObjectives: Understand the need for measurement and systems of units.
Distinguish between fundamental and derived SI units.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify physical equations.
Define frame of reference and describe motion using differentiation and integration.
Distinguish between uniform and non-uniform motion.
Calculate average speed, average velocity, and instantaneous velocity.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs for uniformly accelerated motion.
Perform vector operations including addition, subtraction, and resolution.
Analyze projectile motion and uniform circular motion kinematically.
Apply scalar and vector products to physical problems.
Explain Newton's three laws and the concept of inertia.

topics: Electric charges and Conservation of charge
subtopics: Coulomb's law, forces between multiple charges, superposition principle, continuous charge distribution
key_concepts: Electric field, electric dipole, torque on a dipole
Gauss's theorem and its applications (wire, plane sheet, spherical shell)

subtopics: Potential difference, due to point charge/dipole/system of charges, equipotential surfaces
key_concepts: Capacitors: capacitance, series/parallel combinations, parallel plate capacitor with dielectric, energy stored

subtopics: Drift velocity, mobility, V-I characteristics (linear/non-linear), resistivity/conductivity, temperature dependence of resistance
key_concepts: Internal resistance, emf vs potential difference
Combination of cells, Kirchhoff's rules, Wheatstone bridge

units.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

subtopics: Oersted's experiment, Biot-Savart law (circular loop), Ampere's law (wire/solenoid)
key_concepts: Force on moving charge and current-carrying conductor
Torque on current loop, magnetic dipole moment, galvanometer conversion

subtopics: Bar magnet as solenoid, magnetic dipole field (qualitative), torque on dipole

units.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

subtopics: Faraday's laws, induced EMF and current, Lenz's Law
Peak/RMS values, reactance/impedance, LCR series circuit (phasors), resonance
key_concepts: Power in AC circuits, power factor

subtopics: Displacement current, characteristics, transverse nature (qualitative)
key_concepts: Electromagnetic spectrum and uses of different regions

long_chapter_names_reclassified: Chapter–14: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

practicals.title: To measure diameter of a small spherical/cylindrical body and to measure internal diameter and depth of a given beaker/calorimeter using Vernier Callipers and hence find its volume.
To measure diameter of a given wire and thickness of a given sheet using screw gauge.
To determine volume of an irregular lamina using screw gauge.
To determine radius of curvature of a given spherical surface by a spherometer.
To determine the mass of two different objects using a beam balance.
To find the weight of a given body using parallelogram law of vectors.
Using a simple pendulum, plot its graph and use it to find the effective length of second's pendulum.
To study variation of time period of a simple pendulum of a given length by taking bobs of same size but different masses and interpret the result.
To study the relationship between force of limiting friction and normal reaction and to find the co- efficient of friction between a block and a horizontal surface.

To find the downward force, along an inclined plane, acting on a roller due to gravitational pull of the earth and study its relationship with the angle of inclination θ by plotting graph between force and Sinθ.
To determine Young's modulus of elasticity of the material of a given wire.
To find the force constant of a helical spring by plotting a graph between load and extension.
To study the variation in volume with pressure for a sample of air at constant temperature by plotting graphs between P and V, and between P and 1/V.
To determine the surface tension of water by capillary rise method.
To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a given viscous liquid by measuring terminal velocity of a given spherical body.
To study the relationship between the temperature of a hot body and time by plotting a cooling curve.
To determine specific heat capacity of a given solid by method of mixtures.
To study the relation between frequency and length of a given wire under constant tension using sonometer.
To study the relation between the length of a given wire and tension for constant frequency using sonometer.
To find the speed of sound in air at room temperature using a resonance tube by two resonance positions.

activities.title: To make a paper scale of given least count, e.g., 0.2cm, 0.5 cm.
To determine mass of a given body using a metre scale by principle of moments.
To plot a graph for a given set of data, with proper choice of scales and error bars.
To measure the force of limiting friction for rolling of a roller on a horizontal plane.
To study the variation in range of a projectile with angle of projection.
To study the conservation of energy of a ball rolling down on an inclined plane (using a double inclined plane).
To study dissipation of energy of a simple pendulum by plotting a graph between square of amplitude and time.
To observe change of state and plot a cooling curve for molten wax.
To observe and explain the effect of heating on a bi-metallic strip.
To note the change in level of liquid in a container on heating and interpret the observations.
To study the effect of detergent on surface tension of water by observing capillary rise.
To study the factors affecting the rate of loss of heat of a liquid.
To study the effect of load on depression of a suitably clamped metre scale loaded at (i) its end (ii) in the middle.
To observe the decrease in pressure with increase in velocity of a fluid.

projects.title: Study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends

To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.
To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.
To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

marks_distribution: Class XI/XII Theory: Max Marks 70, Time 3 hrs
Practicals (Theory + Lab): Max Marks 30, Time 2-3 hrs
competency_weightage: Conceptual understanding emphasized over rote learning
Problem-solving abilities and process skills prioritized
Application of Physics concepts in industrial/technological contexts
internal_assessment: Content excluded from NCERT textbooks not to be tested

evaluation_scheme: Two experiments (one from each section): 14 Marks (7+7)
requirements: Record of at least 8 Experiments (4 from Section A, 4 from Section B)
Record of at least 6 Activities (3 from Section A, 3 from Section B)

components: Written test (15 questions, answer any 10): 10 Marks
Practical Record (at least 5 experiments recorded): 5 Marks

examples: Study factors on internal resistance/EMF of a cell
Variations in current with LDR illumination
Refractive indices using plane mirror and lens

practicals.title: To measure diameter of a small spherical/cylindrical body and to measure internal diameter and depth of a given beaker/calorimeter using Vernier Callipers and hence find its volume.
To measure diameter of a given wire and thickness of a given sheet using screw gauge.
To determine volume of an irregular lamina using screw gauge.
To determine radius of curvature of a given spherical surface by a spherometer.
To determine the mass of two different objects using a beam balance.
To find the weight of a given body using parallelogram law of vectors.
Using a simple pendulum, plot its graph and use it to find the effective length of second's pendulum.
To study variation of time period of a simple pendulum of a given length by taking bobs of same size but different masses and interpret the result.
To study the relationship between force of limiting friction and normal reaction and to find the co- efficient of friction between a block and a horizontal surface.

To find the downward force, along an inclined plane, acting on a roller due to gravitational pull of the earth and study its relationship with the angle of inclination θ by plotting graph between force and Sinθ.
To determine Young's modulus of elasticity of the material of a given wire.
To find the force constant of a helical spring by plotting a graph between load and extension.
To study the variation in volume with pressure for a sample of air at constant temperature by plotting graphs between P and V, and between P and 1/V.
To determine the surface tension of water by capillary rise method.
To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a given viscous liquid by measuring terminal velocity of a given spherical body.
To study the relationship between the temperature of a hot body and time by plotting a cooling curve.
To determine specific heat capacity of a given solid by method of mixtures.
To study the relation between frequency and length of a given wire under constant tension using sonometer.
To study the relation between the length of a given wire and tension for constant frequency using sonometer.
To find the speed of sound in air at room temperature using a resonance tube by two resonance positions.

activities.title: To make a paper scale of given least count, e.g., 0.2cm, 0.5 cm.
To determine mass of a given body using a metre scale by principle of moments.
To plot a graph for a given set of data, with proper choice of scales and error bars.
To measure the force of limiting friction for rolling of a roller on a horizontal plane.
To study the variation in range of a projectile with angle of projection.
To study the conservation of energy of a ball rolling down on an inclined plane (using a double inclined plane).
To study dissipation of energy of a simple pendulum by plotting a graph between square of amplitude and time.
To observe change of state and plot a cooling curve for molten wax.
To observe and explain the effect of heating on a bi-metallic strip.
To note the change in level of liquid in a container on heating and interpret the observations.
To study the effect of detergent on surface tension of water by observing capillary rise.
To study the factors affecting the rate of loss of heat of a liquid.
To study the effect of load on depression of a suitably clamped metre scale loaded at (i) its end (ii) in the middle.
To observe the decrease in pressure with increase in velocity of a fluid.

projects.title: Study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends

To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.
To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.
To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

marks_distribution: Class XI/XII Theory: Max Marks 70, Time 3 hrs
Practicals (Theory + Lab): Max Marks 30, Time 2-3 hrs
competency_weightage: Conceptual understanding emphasized over rote learning
Problem-solving abilities and process skills prioritized
Application of Physics concepts in industrial/technological contexts
internal_assessment: Content excluded from NCERT textbooks not to be tested

evaluation_scheme: Two experiments (one from each section): 14 Marks (7+7)
requirements: Record of at least 8 Experiments (4 from Section A, 4 from Section B)
Record of at least 6 Activities (3 from Section A, 3 from Section B)

components: Written test (15 questions, answer any 10): 10 Marks
Practical Record (at least 5 experiments recorded): 5 Marks

examples: Study factors on internal resistance/EMF of a cell
Variations in current with LDR illumination
Refractive indices using plane mirror and lens

long_chapter_names_reclassified: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
Mechanical Properties of Solids
Mechanical Properties of Fluids
Chapter–14: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits

competency_groups.title: Physical World and Measurement - Units and Measurements
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to measurement systems, SI units, significant figures, uncertainty analysis, dimensional analysis, and physical quantities.
competencies: Understand the need for measurement and distinguish between fundamental and derived units within various systems of measurement.
Apply SI unit conventions correctly in all physics formulations and calculations.
Determine appropriate significant figures and calculate uncertainty in experimental results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify physical equations and derive relationships between quantities.

competency_groups.title: Kinematics - Motion in a Straight Line
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to describing motion using calculus, analyzing uniform/non-uniform motion, interpreting kinematic graphs, and understanding relative velocity.
competencies: Define frame of reference and describe rectilinear motion using differentiation and integration techniques.
Distinguish between average speed/velocity and instantaneous velocity in non-uniform motion scenarios.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs to analyze uniformly accelerated motion.
Derive and apply kinematic equations for constant acceleration using graphical methods.

competency_groups.title: Kinematics - Motion in a Plane
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to vector algebra, resolution of vectors, projectile motion, and uniform circular motion.
competencies: Perform scalar and vector operations including addition, subtraction, dot product, and cross product.
Resolve vectors into rectangular components and apply them to two-dimensional problems.
Analyze projectile motion by separating horizontal and vertical components of velocity and acceleration.
Derive equations for uniform circular motion and calculate centripetal force requirements.

competency_groups.title: Laws of Motion - Newton's Laws and Friction
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to force concepts, conservation laws, equilibrium conditions, friction dynamics, and circular motion.
competencies: Apply Newton's three laws of motion to solve problems involving forces and momentum.
Utilize the law of conservation of linear momentum in collision scenarios.
Analyze static and kinetic friction, including rolling friction and lubrication effects.
Determine conditions for equilibrium of concurrent forces and rigid bodies.

competency_groups.title: Work, Energy and Power - Conservation Principles
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to work-energy theorem, potential energy concepts, conservative forces, power calculation, and collision analysis.
competencies: Calculate work done by constant and variable forces using integration or graphical methods.
Apply the Work-Energy Theorem to relate net work to changes in kinetic energy.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces and analyze potential energy functions.
Analyze elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions.

competency_groups.title: Motion of System of Particles - Rotational Dynamics
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to center of mass concepts, torque, angular momentum conservation, rotational kinematics, and moment of inertia.
competencies: Calculate the center of mass for particle systems and rigid bodies like uniform rods.
Apply laws of conservation of linear and angular momentum in isolated systems.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for rotating rigid bodies under external torques.
Compare rotational motion equations with their translational counterparts.
competency_groups.unit_name: Motion of System of Particles and Rigid Body
competency_groups.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion

competency_groups.title: Gravitation - Universal Laws and Orbits
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Kepler's laws, gravitational fields, potential energy variations, orbital mechanics, and escape velocity.
competencies: Apply Kepler's laws of planetary motion to describe celestial trajectories.
Calculate acceleration due to gravity at varying altitudes and depths using universal gravitation law.
Determine gravitational potential energy and potential for point masses and spherical shells.
Derive expressions for orbital velocity, escape speed, and total energy of orbiting satellites.

competency_groups.title: Properties of Bulk Matter - Solids and Fluids
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to elasticity, stress-strain relationships, fluid statics/dynamics, viscosity, surface tension, and thermal expansion.
competencies: Analyze elastic behavior using Hooke's law, Young's modulus, bulk modulus, shear modulus, and Poisson's ratio.
Apply Pascal's law to hydraulic systems and calculate pressure variations in fluid columns.
Utilize Bernoulli's theorem for streamline flow applications like Torricelli's law and dynamic lift.
Explain surface tension phenomena including capillary rise, excess pressure, and wetting angles.

competency_groups.title: Thermal Properties - Heat Transfer and Expansion
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to heat capacity, calorimetry, phase changes, modes of heat transfer, and blackbody radiation laws.
competencies: Differentiate between temperature and heat; calculate specific heat capacities using calorimetry principles.
Analyze thermal expansion in solids, liquids, and gases including anomalous behavior of water.
Explain mechanisms of conduction, convection, and radiation with reference to thermal conductivity.
Apply Wien's displacement law and Stefan-Boltzmann law to blackbody radiation problems.

competency_groups.title: Thermodynamics - Laws and Processes
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to thermal equilibrium, laws of thermodynamics, state variables, and process analysis.
competencies: Define temperature using the Zeroth law and distinguish between heat, work, and internal energy via the First Law.
Apply the Second Law of Thermodynamics to analyze reversible/irreversible processes and cyclic engines.
Analyze isothermal, adiabatic, and other thermodynamic state changes in gases.

competency_groups.title: Kinetic Theory - Gas Behavior
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to the kinetic theory of gases, molecular speed distributions, degrees of freedom, and specific heat capacities.
competencies: Derive the equation of state for a perfect gas from kinetic assumptions regarding pressure.
competency_groups.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases

competency_groups.title: Oscillations - Simple Harmonic Motion
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to periodic motion, SHM equations, energy in oscillators, and pendulum dynamics.
competencies: Identify characteristics of simple harmonic motion (SHM) including phase, frequency, and displacement functions.

competency_groups.title: Waves - Wave Motion and Superposition
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to wave types, superposition principle, standing waves, beats, and interference phenomena.
competencies: Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves and describe progressive wave properties.

competency_groups.title: Electrostatics - Charges, Fields, and Flux
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Coulomb's law, electric fields of various charge distributions, Gauss's theorem applications, dipoles, and potential energy.
competencies: Apply superposition principle and Coulomb's law to calculate forces between multiple point charges.

competency_groups.title: Electrostatic Potential - Capacitance and Dielectrics
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to electric potential, equipotential surfaces, dielectric properties, capacitor combinations, and energy storage.
competencies: Calculate electric potential due to point charges, dipoles, and systems of charges.

competency_groups.title: Current Electricity - Ohm's Law and Circuits
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to drift velocity, resistivity, temperature dependence of resistance, internal resistance, Kirchhoff's laws, and Wheatstone bridge.
competencies: Analyze V-I characteristics for linear and non-linear conductors.

competency_groups.title: Magnetism - Moving Charges and Magnetic Fields
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Biot-Savart law, Ampere's law, forces on charges/conductors, torque on loops, galvanometers, and magnetic dipoles.
competencies: Apply Biot-Savart and Ampere's laws to calculate magnetic fields for wires, solenoids, and loops.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

competency_groups.title: Magnetism - Materials and Properties
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to magnetic properties of materials (para/dia/ferro), magnetization, temperature effects on magnetism, and bar magnets.
competencies: Classify materials based on their magnetic susceptibility and explain the behavior of paramagnetic, diamagnetic, and ferromagnetic substances.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism

competency_groups.title: Electromagnetic Induction - Laws and Applications
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Faraday's laws, Lenz's law, self/mutual induction, AC circuits (reactance/impedance), resonance, power factor, generators, and transformers.
competencies: Apply Faraday's and Lenz's laws to determine induced EMF and current direction.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

competency_groups.title: AC Circuits - Analysis and Power
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to RMS values, reactance/impedance in LCR circuits, resonance conditions, power calculations, AC generators, and transformers.
competencies: Analyze series LCR circuits using phasor diagrams to determine impedance and phase difference.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents

competency_groups.title: Electromagnetic Waves - Spectrum and Properties
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to displacement current, characteristics of EM waves (transverse nature), electromagnetic spectrum regions, and their applications.
competencies: Describe the transverse nature of electromagnetic waves qualitatively.

competency_groups.title: Ray Optics - Reflection, Refraction, and Instruments
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to spherical mirrors/lenses, total internal reflection, optical fibers, prism refraction, magnification, power of lenses, microscopes, and telescopes.
competencies: Apply mirror formula and lens maker's formula to solve image formation problems.
competency_groups.chapter_name: Chapter–9: Ray Optics and Optical Instruments

competency_groups.title: Wave Optics - Interference, Diffraction, and Huygens' Principle
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to wave fronts, interference (Young's experiment), diffraction patterns, coherence, and qualitative treatment of single slit diffraction.
competencies: Apply Huygen's principle to derive laws of reflection and refraction.

competency_groups.title: Dual Nature - Photoelectric Effect and Matter Waves
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to photoelectric effect observations, Einstein's equation, particle nature of light, de-Broglie relation, and matter waves.
competencies: Explain the experimental evidence for the dual nature of radiation.
competency_groups.unit_name: Unit–VII Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
competency_groups.chapter_name: Chapter–11: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

competency_groups.title: Atoms - Models and Spectra
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to Rutherford's scattering experiment, Bohr model postulates (radius/velocity/energy), hydrogen line spectra, nuclear composition, binding energy, fission, and fusion.
competencies: Describe the limitations of classical physics that led to the development of atomic models.

competency_groups.title: Nuclear Physics - Reactions and Energy
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to nuclear forces, mass-energy equivalence, binding energy curves, fission processes, fusion reactions, and radioactive decay concepts.
competencies: Analyze the variation of binding energy per nucleon with mass number.

competency_groups.title: Electronic Devices - Semiconductors and Diodes
competency_groups.description: Competencies related to energy bands, intrinsic/extrinsic semiconductors (p/n type), p-n junction characteristics, rectification applications.
competencies: Explain the formation of depletion region in a p-n junction.

Practicals (Theory + Lab): Max Marks 30, Time 2-3 hrs
competency_weightage: Conceptual understanding emphasized over rote learning
Problem-solving abilities and process skills prioritized
Application of Physics concepts in industrial/technological contexts

Record of at least 6 Activities (3 from Section A, 3 from Section B)

Practical Record (at least 5 experiments recorded): 5 Marks

Variations in current with LDR illumination
Refractive indices using plane mirror and lens

learning_outcomes.topic: Need for measurement, SI units, Significant figures, Dimensional analysis
outcomes: Understand the need for measurement and systems of units.
Distinguish between fundamental and derived SI units.
Apply rules for significant figures and determine uncertainty in results.
Perform dimensional analysis to verify physical equations.

learning_outcomes.topic: Frame of reference, Differentiation/Integration for motion, Graphs
outcomes: Define frame of reference and describe motion using differentiation and integration.
Distinguish between uniform and non-uniform motion.
Calculate average speed, average velocity, and instantaneous velocity.
Interpret position-time and velocity-time graphs for uniformly accelerated motion.

learning_outcomes.topic: Vectors, Projectile Motion, Uniform Circular Motion
outcomes: Perform vector operations including addition, subtraction, and resolution.
Analyze projectile motion and uniform circular motion kinematically.
Apply scalar and vector products to physical problems.

learning_outcomes.topic: Newton's Laws, Momentum, Friction, Circular Dynamics
outcomes: Explain Newton's three laws and the concept of inertia.
Apply conservation of linear momentum to collision problems.
Analyze equilibrium conditions for concurrent forces.
Differentiate between static, kinetic, and rolling friction.

learning_outcomes.topic: Work-Energy Theorem, Potential Energy, Collisions
outcomes: Calculate work done by constant and variable forces.
Apply the Work-Energy theorem to solve motion problems.
Distinguish between conservative and non-conservative forces.
Analyze elastic and inelastic collisions.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Motion of System of Particles and Rigid Body
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
learning_outcomes.topic: Centre of Mass, Torque, Angular Momentum, Moment of Inertia
outcomes: Determine the centre of mass for particle systems and rigid bodies.
Apply conservation laws for linear momentum and angular momentum.
Analyze rotational motion equations and equilibrium of rigid bodies.
Calculate moment of inertia for simple geometrical objects.

learning_outcomes.topic: Kepler's Laws, Universal Gravitation, Orbital Motion
outcomes: State and apply Kepler's laws of planetary motion.
Apply the universal law of gravitation to calculate forces.
Analyze variations in acceleration due to gravity with altitude and depth.
Calculate orbital velocity and escape speed.

learning_outcomes.topic: Elasticity, Stress-Strain, Hooke's Law
outcomes: Define stress and strain and explain the stress-strain relationship.
Apply Hooke's law to calculate Young's modulus, bulk modulus, and shear modulus.
Understand Poisson's ratio and elastic energy.

learning_outcomes.topic: Fluid Pressure, Viscosity, Bernoulli's Theorem, Surface Tension
outcomes: Apply Pascal's law and calculate pressure due to fluid columns.
Explain viscosity, Stokes' law, and terminal velocity.
Apply Bernoulli's theorem to practical situations like Torricelli's law.

learning_outcomes.topic: Heat, Temperature, Thermal Expansion, Heat Transfer
outcomes: Distinguish between heat and temperature.
Calculate specific heat capacity and latent heat during state changes.
Explain modes of heat transfer: conduction, convection, and radiation.

learning_outcomes.topic: Laws of Thermodynamics, Processes
outcomes: Define temperature using the Zeroth law.
Apply the First and Second laws of thermodynamics to various processes.
Distinguish between isothermal, adiabatic, reversible, and irreversible processes.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Behaviour of Perfect Gases and Kinetic Theory of Gases
learning_outcomes.topic: Equation of State, Molecular Speeds, Degrees of Freedom
outcomes: Derive the equation of state for a perfect gas.
Explain kinetic theory assumptions and the concept of pressure.
Relate temperature to mean kinetic energy and rms speed.

learning_outcomes.topic: Periodic Motion, Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)
outcomes: Define periodic motion and SHM equations.
Analyze energy in simple harmonic motion.
Derive the time period of a simple pendulum.

learning_outcomes.topic: Wave Motion, Superposition, Standing Waves, Beats
outcomes: Distinguish between transverse and longitudinal waves.
Apply the principle of superposition to standing waves in strings and pipes.
Explain the phenomenon of beats.

learning_outcomes.topic: Coulomb's Law, Superposition Principle, Gauss's Theorem
outcomes: Apply Coulomb's law to calculate forces between point charges.
Use the superposition principle for multiple charge systems.
Apply Gauss's theorem to find electric fields of symmetric distributions.

learning_outcomes.topic: Electric Potential, Equipotential Surfaces, Capacitors
outcomes: Calculate electric potential due to point charges and dipoles.
Explain the concept of equipotential surfaces.
Analyze series and parallel combinations of capacitors.

learning_outcomes.topic: Ohm's Law, Resistivity, Kirchhoff's Rules
outcomes: Relate drift velocity to electric current.
Apply Ohm's law and analyze V-I characteristics.
Use Kirchhoff's rules to solve complex circuits.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
learning_outcomes.topic: Biot-Savart Law, Ampere's Law, Force on Conductors
outcomes: Apply Biot-Savart law to calculate magnetic fields.
Use Ampere's law for symmetric current distributions.
Calculate force and torque on moving charges and current loops.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-III Magnetic Effects of Current and Magnetism
learning_outcomes.topic: Magnetic Properties, Dipoles, Electromagnets
outcomes: Classify materials as para-, dia-, or ferro-magnetic.
Analyze the behavior of magnetic dipoles in external fields.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
learning_outcomes.topic: Faraday's Laws, Lenz's Law, Self/Mutual Induction
outcomes: State Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction.
Apply Lenz's law to determine the direction of induced current.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit-IV Electromagnetic Induction and Alternating Currents
learning_outcomes.topic: AC Circuits, Reactance, Resonance, Power Factor
outcomes: Analyze LCR series circuits using phasors.
Calculate resonance conditions and power factor in AC circuits.

learning_outcomes.topic: Displacement Current, EM Spectrum
outcomes: Explain the concept of displacement current.
Identify regions of the electromagnetic spectrum and their uses.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Chapter–9: Ray Optics and Optical Instruments
learning_outcomes.topic: Reflection, Refraction, Lenses, Mirrors, Optical Instruments
outcomes: Apply mirror and lens formulas to image formation.
Analyze total internal reflection and optical fibers.
Calculate magnifying power of microscopes and telescopes.

learning_outcomes.topic: Huygen's Principle, Interference, Diffraction
outcomes: Apply Huygen's principle to derive laws of reflection and refraction.
Analyze Young's double slit experiment for interference patterns.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Unit–VII Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Chapter–11: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
learning_outcomes.topic: Photoelectric Effect, de-Broglie Relation
outcomes: Explain the photoelectric effect using Einstein's equation.
Apply the de-Broglie relation to matter waves.

learning_outcomes.topic: Rutherford Model, Bohr Model, Hydrogen Spectrum
outcomes: Describe the Rutherford alpha-particle scattering experiment.
Apply Bohr's model to calculate radius and energy of orbits.

learning_outcomes.topic: Nuclear Composition, Binding Energy, Fission/Fusion
outcomes: Explain mass defect and binding energy per nucleon.
Differentiate between nuclear fission and fusion processes.

learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Chapter–14: Semiconductor Electronics: Materials, Devices and Simple Circuits
learning_outcomes.topic: Semiconductors, p-n Junction Diode, Rectifiers
outcomes: Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors.
Analyze I-V characteristics of a p-n junction diode in forward and reverse bias.

activities.title: To make a paper scale of given least count, e.g., 0.2cm, 0.5 cm.
To determine mass of a given body using a metre scale by principle of moments.
To plot a graph for a given set of data, with proper choice of scales and error bars.
To measure the force of limiting friction for rolling of a roller on a horizontal plane.
To study the variation in range of a projectile with angle of projection.
To study the conservation of energy of a ball rolling down on an inclined plane (using a double inclined plane).
To study dissipation of energy of a simple pendulum by plotting a graph between square of amplitude and time.
To observe change of state and plot a cooling curve for molten wax.
To observe and explain the effect of heating on a bi-metallic strip.
To note the change in level of liquid in a container on heating and interpret the observations.
To study the effect of detergent on surface tension of water by observing capillary rise.
To study the factors affecting the rate of loss of heat of a liquid.
To study the effect of load on depression of a suitably clamped metre scale loaded at (i) its end (ii) in the middle.
To observe the decrease in pressure with increase in velocity of a fluid.

projects.title: Study various factors on which the internal resistance/EMF of a cell depends

To study the variations in current flowing in a circuit containing an LDR because of a variation in (a) the power of the incandescent lamp, used to 'illuminate' the LDR (keeping all the lamps at a fixed distance). (b) the distance of an incandescent lamp (of fixed power) used to 'illuminate' the LDR.
To find the refractive indices of (a) water (b) oil (transparent) using a plane mirror, an equiconvex lens (made from a glass of known refractive index) and an adjustable object needle.
To investigate the relation between the ratio of (i) output and input voltage and (ii) number of turns in the secondary coil and primary coil of a self-designed transformer.
To investigate the dependence of the angle of deviation on the angle of incidence using a hollow prism filled one by one, with different transparent fluids.
To estimate the charge induced on each one of the two identical Styrofoam (or pith) balls suspended in a vertical plane by making use of Coulomb's law.

To study the factor on which the self-inductance of a coil depends by observing the effect of this coil, when put in series with a resistor/(bulb) in a circuit fed up by an A.C. source of adjustable frequency.
To study the earth's magnetic field using a compass needle -bar magnet by plotting magnetic field lines and tangent galvanometer.

practicals.title: To measure diameter of a small spherical/cylindrical body and to measure internal diameter and depth of a given beaker/calorimeter using Vernier Callipers and hence find its volume.
To measure diameter of a given wire and thickness of a given sheet using screw gauge.
To determine volume of an irregular lamina using screw gauge.
To determine radius of curvature of a given spherical surface by a spherometer.
To determine the mass of two different objects using a beam balance.
To find the weight of a given body using parallelogram law of vectors.
Using a simple pendulum, plot its graph and use it to find the effective length of second's pendulum.
To study variation of time period of a simple pendulum of a given length by taking bobs of same size but different masses and interpret the result.
To study the relationship between force of limiting friction and normal reaction and to find the co- efficient of friction between a block and a horizontal surface.

To find the downward force, along an inclined plane, acting on a roller due to gravitational pull of the earth and study its relationship with the angle of inclination θ by plotting graph between force and Sinθ.
To determine Young's modulus of elasticity of the material of a given wire.
To find the force constant of a helical spring by plotting a graph between load and extension.
To study the variation in volume with pressure for a sample of air at constant temperature by plotting graphs between P and V, and between P and 1/V.
To determine the surface tension of water by capillary rise method.
To determine the coefficient of viscosity of a given viscous liquid by measuring terminal velocity of a given spherical body.
To study the relationship between the temperature of a hot body and time by plotting a cooling curve.
To determine specific heat capacity of a given solid by method of mixtures.
To study the relation between frequency and length of a given wire under constant tension using sonometer.
To study the relation between the length of a given wire and tension for constant frequency using sonometer.
To find the speed of sound in air at room temperature using a resonance tube by two resonance positions.
